title: Stability and change
summary: Ecosystems change all the time, yet many hold a recognisable structure for decades or centuries. This lesson builds the whole D4.2 picture of why: the factors that make an ecosystem stable (biodiversity, genetic diversity, complex food webs, climatic conditions and nutrient availability), the difference between resistance and resilience, tipping points and alternative stable states, the disproportionate role of keystone species, ecological succession from pioneers to a climax community, and how human disturbance is detected through long-term monitoring. Every explanation is set out the way our marking engine actually awards Paper 2 marks — one mark per distinct valid point, with error-carried-forward — so you can see how to phrase an answer that scores.
learningObjectives: Identify the factors that contribute to the stability of an ecosystem: biodiversity, genetic diversity, complex food webs, climatic conditions and the availability of nutrients.
Distinguish resistance from resilience, and explain a tipping point as a threshold beyond which an ecosystem shifts to a different stable state.
Explain the disproportionate role of a keystone species in maintaining community structure.
Describe ecological succession as directional change, distinguishing primary from secondary succession and the sequence from pioneer to climax community.
Explain how human disturbance destabilises ecosystems and how long-term monitoring detects change.
Present an answer so that each distinct valid point earns a mark and error-carried-forward protects the reasoning that follows.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Why some ecosystems bend without breaking
simpleExplanation.summary: A stable ecosystem keeps roughly the same species and structure over time even when conditions wobble. Stability comes from having many interconnected parts: lots of species, lots of genetic variation within them, tangled food webs, a steady climate and a reliable nutrient supply. Push too hard, though, and the system can flip past a tipping point into a completely different state that is hard to reverse.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a suspension bridge held up by hundreds of cables. If one cable frays, the load simply redistributes across the others and the deck stays level — that redundancy is what biodiversity and complex food webs give an ecosystem. But keep cutting cables and you eventually reach a threshold where the next cut sends the whole deck plunging into a new, collapsed shape it will not spring back from. That threshold is the tipping point, and the collapsed shape is an alternative stable state.
steps: List the stabilising factors: high biodiversity, high genetic diversity within species, complex food webs, a stable climate and a steady nutrient supply all make an ecosystem harder to shift.
Separate two ideas: resistance is staying unchanged when disturbed; resilience is recovering after being disturbed.
Understand a tipping point as a threshold — cross it and the ecosystem reorganises into a different stable state, often held there by its own feedback.

See directional change over time as succession: bare ground is colonised by pioneers, soil and shelter build up, and a sequence of communities leads towards a climax community. Human disturbance can reset or derail this, which is why we monitor ecosystems to detect change early.

flashcards.back: The tendency of an ecosystem to keep a broadly constant structure, species composition and function over time despite disturbance. It is supported by biodiversity, genetic diversity, complex food webs, a stable climate and a steady nutrient supply.
The ability of an ecosystem to withstand a disturbance and remain essentially unchanged — for example a diverse forest shrugging off a pest outbreak that would devastate a monoculture.

The ability and speed of an ecosystem to recover to its former state after a disturbance — for example grassland regrowing after a fire. High biodiversity and genetic diversity tend to raise resilience.

More species means more complex food webs with alternative feeding pathways, so the loss of one species can be compensated for by others (functional redundancy). The ecosystem therefore both resists and recovers from disturbance more effectively.

Greater genetic variation within a species means a higher chance that some individuals carry alleles conferring tolerance to a new stress (disease, heat, drought), so the population can adapt and persist rather than collapse.
A threshold beyond which an ecosystem shifts to a different, self-reinforcing stable state. Small further change near the threshold triggers a large, often abrupt and hard-to-reverse switch.
A different configuration an ecosystem settles into after crossing a tipping point, held in place by its own feedback — for example a clear lake becoming a turbid, algae-dominated one.

A species with an effect on community structure far greater than its abundance would suggest. Removing it causes disproportionate change and can collapse the community, even though it may be uncommon.
The directional, more-or-less predictable change in the community of an area over time, running from colonisation by pioneer species towards a relatively stable climax community.

Primary succession begins on newly exposed ground with NO soil (bare rock, lava, glacial retreat) and is slow because soil must form. Secondary succession begins where a disturbance removed the community but LEFT the soil (after a fire or on abandoned farmland) and is faster.

The first colonisers of bare ground, such as lichens and mosses. They tolerate harsh conditions, weather rock and trap organic matter, beginning soil formation and making the site habitable for later species.
The relatively stable community that persists at the end of a successional sequence for a given climate and soil, in dynamic equilibrium with its environment until the next major disturbance.

Repeated, standardised measurement of an ecosystem over time (e.g. species counts, cover, water chemistry) that establishes a baseline and detects change — often early warning of approaching instability or a tipping point.

Resistance is NOT changing when disturbed; resilience is BOUNCING BACK after change. An ecosystem can have high resistance but low resilience (a coral reef) or the reverse. Do not use the words interchangeably.

A keystone species is defined by its OUTSIZE effect on community structure, not by being numerous or the biggest. A dominant species is abundant; a keystone species matters far more than its abundance predicts.

quickCheck.prompt: Which statement best explains WHY high biodiversity tends to increase the stability of an ecosystem?
options: More species means the ecosystem contains more total biomass, which is always more stable
More species produce more complex food webs with alternative feeding pathways, so the loss of one species can be compensated for
More species means competition is eliminated, so nothing ever changes
More species guarantees that no species can ever go extinct
quickCheck.answer: More species produce more complex food webs with alternative feeding pathways, so the loss of one species can be compensated for

quickCheck.prompt: A keystone species is best described as a species that:
options: Is the most abundant organism in the community
Has the largest body size in the community
Has an effect on community structure far greater than its abundance would suggest
Sits at the base of the food web as a producer
quickCheck.answer: Has an effect on community structure far greater than its abundance would suggest

quickCheck.prompt: An ecosystem crosses a tipping point. This means that:
options: It has been disturbed but will recover to exactly its original state
It has passed a threshold and shifted to a different, self-reinforcing stable state that is hard to reverse
Its temperature has risen by a fixed amount
All of its species have gone extinct at once
quickCheck.answer: It has passed a threshold and shifted to a different, self-reinforcing stable state that is hard to reverse

quickCheck.prompt: Which situation is an example of PRIMARY (not secondary) succession?
options: Regrowth of a forest on farmland that has been abandoned
Recolonisation of a woodland after a fire that left the soil intact
Colonisation of bare rock exposed by a retreating glacier
A pond gradually refilling with fish after a drought
quickCheck.answer: Colonisation of bare rock exposed by a retreating glacier

quickCheck.prompt: Why does greater genetic diversity within a species contribute to ecosystem stability?
options: It makes every individual identical, so the population responds uniformly
It increases the chance that some individuals tolerate a new stress such as disease, so the population can persist and adapt
It removes the need for natural selection
It automatically increases the number of species present
quickCheck.answer: It increases the chance that some individuals tolerate a new stress such as disease, so the population can persist and adapt

faq.q: Is high biodiversity always linked to high stability?
faq.a: As a general rule higher biodiversity supports greater stability, and IB expects you to be able to explain the mechanism: more species build more complex food webs with alternative feeding pathways, so if one species declines others can take over its role (functional redundancy), giving both resistance and resilience. The relationship is not a rigid law — it depends on the ecosystem and the kind of disturbance — but the mechanistic reasoning is what earns marks, so always explain HOW diversity helps rather than just asserting that it does.

faq.q: What is the difference between a keystone species and a dominant species?
faq.a: A dominant species is simply one that is very abundant or contributes most biomass. A keystone species is defined by its disproportionate effect on community structure: removing it changes the community far more than its numbers would predict. A keystone species can in fact be quite rare, which is exactly why the definition stresses effect relative to abundance rather than abundance itself.

faq.q: Does succession always reach the same climax community?
faq.a: For a given climate and soil, succession tends towards a broadly predictable climax community, but the outcome is not perfectly fixed. Chance events, the order of colonisation, and ongoing disturbance can leave an ecosystem in a different stable configuration, and repeated human disturbance may hold it at an earlier successional stage. Treat the climax as the relatively stable end-point in dynamic equilibrium with its environment, not as an inevitable single destination.

faq.q: Why do scientists monitor ecosystems instead of just responding when something goes wrong?
faq.a: Because tipping points are often crossed abruptly and are hard to reverse afterwards. Long-term, standardised monitoring — repeated species counts, percentage cover, water chemistry and so on — establishes a baseline and reveals trends, such as a keystone predator declining or nutrients creeping upward, while there is still time to intervene. Detecting change early is far cheaper and more effective than trying to restore an ecosystem that has already flipped into an alternative stable state.

sections.content: Walk into an old woodland and it looks changeless, yet it is anything but static: species are constantly born, eaten and replaced while the overall structure holds steady. That capacity to keep a recognisable form despite disturbance is ecosystem stability, and D4.2 asks you both to explain where it comes from and to recognise when it breaks down. In this lesson we build the full picture — the factors that make an ecosystem stable, the difference between resisting and recovering from disturbance, the tipping points that flip a system into a new state, the keystone species that hold communities together, the directional change of succession, and the human disturbance that monitoring is designed to catch. Because most D4.2 marks are for explanation, we also show exactly how our marking engine awards them, point by point.

Stability is not the absence of change — it is the tendency of an ecosystem to keep a broadly constant structure, species composition and function over time despite disturbance. The syllabus identifies five interacting factors that promote it. High biodiversity means many species, which build more complex food webs; genetic diversity within those species widens the pool of tolerances available to meet new stresses; complex food webs provide alternative feeding pathways so the loss of one link can be compensated for; stable climatic conditions avoid pushing organisms beyond their tolerance limits; and a reliable availability of nutrients supports steady productivity throughout the web. Notice how the factors reinforce one another: diversity feeds web complexity, and web complexity is what turns diversity into resistance and resilience.

items: **Biodiversity:** more species → more complex food webs → alternative pathways, so losing one species is buffered (functional redundancy).
**Genetic diversity:** more variation within species → higher chance some individuals tolerate a new stress (disease, heat), so populations adapt rather than collapse.
**Complex food webs:** many interconnected feeding relationships spread the impact of any single change across the community.
**Climatic conditions:** a stable climate keeps organisms within their tolerance limits, avoiding stress that would destabilise populations.
**Nutrient availability:** a steady, balanced nutrient supply sustains productivity; both shortage and excess (e.g. nutrient loading) can destabilise the system.

sections.content: Stability has two distinct components that students routinely blur. Resistance is the ability to withstand a disturbance and stay essentially unchanged — a diverse forest absorbing a pest outbreak. Resilience is the ability, and the speed, to recover to the former state after being disturbed — grassland regrowing after a fire. An ecosystem can be strong in one and weak in the other, so name whichever you mean. The stabilising factors above raise both. But every ecosystem has a limit. A tipping point is a threshold beyond which an ecosystem shifts to a different stable state: near the threshold a small further change can trigger a large, abrupt and often irreversible switch, after which the system is held in its new configuration by its own reinforcing feedback. Recovery is then difficult because the return path differs from the path of degradation — conditions must often be improved far past the original threshold before the ecosystem flips back.

items: **Resistance:** withstanding disturbance without changing.
**Resilience:** recovering to the former state after disturbance, and how fast.
**Tipping point:** a threshold beyond which the ecosystem shifts to a different stable state.
**Alternative stable state:** the new, self-reinforcing configuration reached after crossing the threshold (e.g. clear lake → turbid lake, coral reef → algae-dominated reef).
**Hard to reverse:** feedback holds the new state in place, so restoration usually needs conditions far better than those at which the tipping point occurred.

sections.content: Not all species matter equally to community structure. A keystone species has an effect on the community far greater than its abundance would suggest: remove it and the whole community reorganises, even if the species was never numerous. The classic mechanism is top-down control by a predator. Where a predator keeps a strong competitor in check, many weaker species coexist alongside it; remove the predator and the released competitor can take over space or resources and drive others out, collapsing diversity. Keystone effects are not limited to predators — a species can be keystone through engineering habitat, dispersing seeds or providing a critical resource — but the defining feature is always disproportion: outsize influence relative to abundance. This is why keystone species are a priority for conservation and monitoring; losing one can tip a whole community.

items: **Definition:** effect on community structure disproportionately large relative to abundance.
**Not the same as dominant:** a dominant species is merely abundant; a keystone species can be rare yet pivotal.
**Typical mechanism:** a keystone predator suppresses a strong competitor, allowing many other species to coexist.
**On removal:** the released competitor dominates, diversity and community structure collapse — a disproportionate effect.
**Consequence:** keystone species are conservation priorities because their loss can push a community past a tipping point.

sections.content: Ecological succession: directional change over time

Alongside disturbance, ecosystems change in a directional way through ecological succession — the more-or-less predictable change in a community over time. In primary succession the process starts on newly exposed ground with no soil at all: bare rock, cooled lava or land uncovered by a retreating glacier. Pioneer species such as lichens and mosses colonise first because they tolerate these harsh conditions; they weather the rock and trap dead organic matter, slowly building the first thin soil. That soil retains water and nutrients, allowing grasses, then shrubs, then trees to establish in turn, each stage modifying conditions in ways that favour the next. The sequence tends towards a relatively stable climax community, in dynamic equilibrium with the prevailing climate and soil. Secondary succession follows the same directional idea but starts where a disturbance — a fire, a flood, or the abandonment of farmland — removed the community yet left the soil intact. Because soil, seeds and nutrients are already present, secondary succession is much faster than primary succession.

items: **Succession is directional:** community change runs predictably from colonisation towards a climax community.
**Primary:** begins on ground with NO soil (rock, lava, glacial retreat); slow, because soil must form first.
**Pioneers → climax:** pioneers (lichens, mosses) modify the site → grasses → shrubs → trees → climax community.
**Facilitation:** each stage alters conditions (soil depth, shade, moisture) in ways that favour the next stage.
**Secondary:** begins where soil REMAINS after disturbance (after fire, on abandoned farmland); much faster than primary.

sections.content: Human activity is a powerful and frequent source of disturbance that erodes the very factors that confer stability. Deforestation and habitat fragmentation lower biodiversity and simplify food webs; overharvesting can remove keystone species; pollution and agricultural runoff overload nutrient supplies and can drive an ecosystem toward a eutrophic tipping point; and climate change shifts the climatic conditions organisms are adapted to. Because such change can be gradual until a threshold is crossed, ecosystems are studied through long-term monitoring — repeated, standardised measurements such as species counts, percentage cover, population sizes and water chemistry. Monitoring establishes a baseline and reveals trends over time, so that a declining keystone population or a slow rise in nutrients can be detected while intervention is still possible, rather than after the system has already flipped into an alternative stable state.

items: **Human disturbances** — deforestation, fragmentation, overharvesting, pollution/nutrient loading, climate change — attack the factors that stabilise ecosystems.
**They can be gradual** until a tipping point is crossed, after which change is abrupt and hard to reverse.
**Monitoring** = repeated, standardised measurement (species counts, cover, population size, water chemistry) against a baseline.
**Purpose:** detect trends and early-warning signals in time to intervene, before an alternative stable state locks in.

sections.question: Two meadows are surveyed and Simpson's reciprocal diversity index, $D = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{\sum n(n-1)}$, is used to compare them, where $N$ is the total number of organisms of all species and $n$ is the number of each species. Meadow A contains five species with counts 10, 12, 8, 15 and 5. Meadow B contains five species with counts 40, 4, 3, 2 and 1. (a) Calculate $D$ for each meadow. (b) State, with a reason, which meadow is likely to be more stable. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a) Meadow A.**
$N = 10+12+8+15+5 = 50$, so $N(N-1) = 50 \times 49 = 2450$.
$\sum n(n-1) = (10\times9)+(12\times11)+(8\times7)+(15\times14)+(5\times4) = 90+132+56+210+20 = 508$.
$D_A = \dfrac{2450}{508} = 4.82$.  [M1: correct method; A1: 4.82]

**Meadow B.**
$N = 40+4+3+2+1 = 50$, so $N(N-1) = 2450$.
$\sum n(n-1) = (40\times39)+(4\times3)+(3\times2)+(2\times1)+(1\times0) = 1560+12+6+2+0 = 1580$.
$D_B = \dfrac{2450}{1580} = 1.55$.  [A1: 1.55]

**(b)** Meadow A is more stable. It has the higher diversity index (4.82 vs 1.55) because its individuals are spread more evenly across species, whereas Meadow B is dominated by one species. Higher biodiversity means a more complex food web with alternative feeding pathways, so the loss of any one species is more easily compensated for. [A1: correct choice with a diversity-based reason]

*Both meadows have the same total organisms and the same species richness (5), yet very different indices — evenness matters, not just how many species are present.*

sections.question: On a rocky shore, ecologists monitor a plot before and after experimentally removing a predatory starfish thought to be a keystone species. Before removal, mussels covered 25% of the rock and 15 animal species were recorded. Two years after removal, mussel cover was 85% and only 8 animal species remained. (a) Calculate the percentage increase in mussel cover and the percentage decrease in species richness. (b) Explain how these data support the claim that the starfish is a keystone species. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a) Change in mussel cover.**
Increase $= \dfrac{85 - 25}{25} \times 100 = \dfrac{60}{25} \times 100 = 240\%$.  [M1: method; A1: 240%]

**Change in species richness.**
Decrease $= \dfrac{15 - 8}{15} \times 100 = \dfrac{7}{15} \times 100 = 47\%$ (to 2 s.f.).  [A1: ≈47%]

**(b)** Removing the starfish caused a disproportionately large change in community structure — mussels (a strong competitor for space) expanded to dominate the rock and nearly halved the number of species present. Because a change in one relatively uncommon predator produced this outsize effect on the whole community, the starfish fits the definition of a keystone species, and the repeated before-and-after survey shows how monitoring detects the change. [A1: links disproportionate effect on community structure to the keystone definition]

*Notice the reasoning: the argument is not that the starfish was abundant, but that its removal changed the community out of all proportion to its numbers.*

items: **Using 'resistance' and 'resilience' as synonyms** — resistance is staying unchanged when disturbed; resilience is recovering afterwards. State which you mean; muddling them loses the mark.

**Asserting 'biodiversity gives stability' with no mechanism** — you must explain HOW: more species → more complex food webs → alternative pathways → loss of one species compensated for. A bare claim scores nothing.

**Defining a keystone species as the most abundant or largest** — it is defined by a disproportionate effect on community structure relative to its abundance; it can be rare. Do not confuse keystone with dominant.

**Describing a tipping point as ordinary disturbance or reversible change** — it is a threshold beyond which the ecosystem shifts to a DIFFERENT, self-reinforcing stable state that is hard to reverse.

**Mixing up primary and secondary succession** — primary starts with NO soil (bare rock/lava/glacial retreat) and is slow; secondary starts where soil REMAINS (after fire, abandoned farmland) and is faster.
**Calling succession 'random change'** — succession is DIRECTIONAL and broadly predictable, from pioneers towards a climax community, each stage facilitating the next.
**Saying a climax community 'never changes'** — it is a relatively stable end-point in dynamic equilibrium, not a frozen, changeless state.
**Treating monitoring as a one-off measurement** — it must be repeated and standardised over time against a baseline to detect trends; a single count reveals no change.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Most D4.2 marks are for explanation, and our engine awards them analytically: each distinct valid point is worth one mark, method/reasoning marks (M) credit the mechanism, answer marks (A) credit a correct conclusion, and error-carried-forward (ECF) means an earlier slip does not cost you the marks that follow if your reasoning is shown. Equivalent correct wording is accepted. Study how each mark below is pinned to a specific, named idea rather than to loose phrasing.

sections.question: Explain how high biodiversity contributes to the stability of an ecosystem. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
High biodiversity means many species are present, which produces a more complex food web with alternative feeding pathways between organisms. Because of these alternative pathways, if one species declines or is lost its role can be taken over by others, so the loss is compensated for and the effect on the community is buffered. Greater diversity is usually accompanied by greater genetic diversity within species, which increases the chance that some individuals can tolerate a new stress such as disease or changing conditions, so populations adapt rather than collapse. Together these mean the ecosystem is better able both to resist a disturbance and to recover from it — it is more stable.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (M):** more species → more complex food web / more alternative feeding pathways.
- **Point 2 (M):** loss (or decline) of one species can be compensated for by others / functional redundancy.
- **Point 3 (M):** greater genetic diversity increases resilience to change / disease / new stress.
- **Point 4 (A):** therefore the ecosystem resists AND recovers from disturbance — i.e. it is more stable (the conclusion of the reasoning chain).

The engine credits **distinct** ideas: restating 'there are more species' in two ways scores once. Marks are for correct reasoning, so a point only counts if the mechanism is stated, not merely the buzzword. **Equivalents are accepted** — 'redundancy', 'other species fill the niche', 'the food web reroutes' all satisfy Point 2.

**ECF and partial credit.** The points are independent. A student who never mentions genetic diversity can still reach 4 by developing the food-web reasoning fully (e.g. more species → alternative pathways → loss compensated → resists → recovers). No point depends on getting an earlier one 'right', so there is no all-or-nothing penalty.

**What the engine rejects.** 'More biodiversity means more stability' on its own scores ZERO — it asserts the conclusion without any mechanism. Likewise a vague 'there is more balance' names no process. The mark scheme wants the causal chain: diversity → complex web → compensation for loss → resistance and recovery.

**Bottom line:** to secure all 4 marks, give three distinct mechanism points (complex food web/alternative pathways → loss compensated for → genetic diversity raises resilience) and close with the conclusion that the ecosystem both resists and recovers, i.e. is more stable.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, crediting each distinct valid idea, applying ECF and capping at the marks available. So write in separate, creditable statements and always give the mechanism: name the complex food web and alternative pathways, say the loss of a species is compensated for, note that genetic diversity raises resilience, then conclude that the ecosystem resists and recovers. A bare claim that 'diversity means stability' persuades the reader but earns nothing — the engine rewards the named reasoning, and showing each step is what protects your score.

The ideas in D4.2 tie directly into the rest of ecology. The diversity and food-web reasoning connects to how populations and communities interact and to trophic relationships; tipping points and feedback reappear in climate and the carbon cycle; and succession, keystone effects and monitoring underpin conservation and the assessment of human impact. Carry one habit across all of it: when a question asks WHY an ecosystem is stable or unstable, answer with a mechanism — which factor, acting through which process, produces which effect — because that causal chain is exactly what the marks are for.
